content.text: ) contains only TypeScript type information — no JavaScript code. It tells the TypeScript compiler the shape of a JavaScript module that already exists at runtime, so you get type checking and IDE auto-complete without duplicating the runtime code in TypeScript.

attrs.alt: Declaration file role: library.js provides runtime code, library.d.ts provides types; tsc reads both and emits output.js + output.d.ts

content.text: TypeScript compiles to JavaScript, erasing all type annotations. When you ship a library, consumers get
files. If they also use TypeScript, they need to know the types. Rather than rewriting the library in TypeScript, you ship a companion
file that describes the API surface. Most npm packages today either ship their own
files or have types in the
files alongside the compiled JavaScript when
is set in tsconfig. Angular libraries generated with
When a JavaScript library has no types, write a
keyword tells TypeScript: "this value exists at runtime — I'm just describing it."

is the standard way to get types for packages that do not bundle their own.
source. IDEs use this to navigate to the source file instead of the generated declaration when you cmd-click on a symbol from a library.

— everything else is private to the library bundle.
) are plain objects. If your app reads custom environment properties, declare them in a
for a publishable library requires several declaration-related settings. Angular libraries generated with
configure these automatically, but understanding each option is important for non-Angular library authors.
in tsconfig prevents that symbol from appearing in the generated
file. This is the TypeScript way to mark implementation details that are not part of the public API.

) are TypeScript-specific single-line comments that appear at the very top of a file. They are the predecessor to ES module imports for pulling in type files, and are still needed in specific scenarios.
Include a specific .ts or .d.ts file by path (legacy multi-file compilation)
Include a TypeScript built-in lib definition (e.g. dom, esnext.asynciterable)
Exclude all default lib files — advanced use only (custom environments)
Declaration files can contain any valid TypeScript type syntax, including conditional types and mapped types. Libraries that expose complex generic utilities produce
files that look very different from their runtime JavaScript.
Not all JavaScript libraries use ES module syntax. CommonJS modules (
) need a different declaration style using
instead of named or default exports.
When esModuleInterop is true (Angular default), TypeScript lets you use default import syntax for export = modules. Without it you must use import chalk = require('chalk').

TypeScript uses structural typing — a type is compatible if it has at least the required shape. Declaration files leverage this to describe the minimum interface that functions accept, making them flexible for consumers while still providing type safety.
Declaration Files in Angular Libraries — Build Pipeline
on an Angular library, the Angular build toolchain runs several steps that all produce
(Angular compiler) transforms templates into TypeScript and runs
packs the output into the Angular Package Format (APF) —
is the entry point for consumers
Debugging Type Errors in Declaration Files
Understanding common type errors that arise from declaration files helps diagnose issues faster:
Symbol not in public-api.ts or wrong .d.ts path
Check package.json typings field and re-export
Type 'X' is not assignable to type 'X'
Two copies of the same @types package in node_modules
Deduplicate with npm dedupe or check peer dependencies
Property 'X' does not exist on type 'Window'
Custom window property needs augmentation in
Unit tests import the same public API that is described in
files. Testing ambient globals or augmented interfaces requires a little extra setup in the test environment.
files contain only type declarations — they have no runtime code and are erased from the final bundle.

content: Ambient declaration files use the declare keyword to describe values that exist at runtime but were not written in TypeScript.

content.text: packages provide community declarations for JavaScript libraries without bundled types.
enables IDE 'go to source' from the .d.ts back to the .ts file.

content: Angular libraries expose their API through public-api.ts — only symbols exported from there appear in the library's .d.ts.
